questions.q: What is the primary mechanism by which gossip protocols achieve information dissemination?
options: Nodes periodically exchange information with randomly selected peers, exponentially increasing the reach of updates until full propagation is achieved
A central server broadcasts information to all nodes simultaneously
Each node sends its information to every other node in the network
Information is disseminated through a predetermined spanning tree
questions.explanation: Gossip spreads via random pairwise exchanges; coverage grows exponentially (like epidemics), not linearly. Option 1 contradicts decentralization. Option 2 assumes point-to-point delivery to all peers. Option 3 requires a precomputed tree, limiting adaptability.

questions.q: Why is the term 'epidemic protocol' used to describe gossip algorithms?
options: Information spreads through a population of nodes in a manner analogous to how diseases propagate through a population via random contacts
Gossip protocols are designed to handle infectious failures in distributed systems
Epidemic protocols require quarantine mechanisms to prevent total system failure
The term refers to the exponential growth of network traffic
questions.explanation: Like epidemics, gossip achieves exponential spread through random interactions (S → I → S model). Option 1 is about resilience, not epidemiology. Option 2 is unrelated to the analogy. Option 3 describes congestion, not epidemiology.

questions.q: What is the 'push' variant of gossip protocols and when is it most effective?
options: A node actively sends information to randomly selected peers; most effective in the early stage of dissemination when few nodes know the update
Nodes passively request information from other nodes, downloading updates on demand
A push variant requires a central server to initiate all information transfers
Push protocols guarantee that all nodes receive information within a bounded time
questions.explanation: Push: sender initiates; effective early because senders are rare. Pull (opposite) is better late when many have the update. Option 1 is about pull/passive requests. Option 2 requires centralization. Option 3 is false; gossip is probabilistic.

questions.q: How does the 'pull' variant of gossip differ from 'push' and why might it be preferred in certain scenarios?
options: Pull: a node requests information from random peers; preferred late in dissemination to reduce load on senders and avoid duplicate sends
Pull variants guarantee lossless information propagation
Pull is more efficient than push in all scenarios
Pull protocols require a central authority to coordinate requests
questions.explanation: Pull spreads load late when many nodes have the update; avoids redundant sends. Option 1 doesn't guarantee losslessness. Option 2 is scenario-dependent. Option 3 requires coordination.

questions.q: What is 'anti-entropy' in gossip protocols and how does it improve fault tolerance?
options: A background process where nodes periodically exchange complete state with peers, allowing nodes that missed previous gossip rounds to recover lost updates
Anti-entropy is a technique that deletes outdated information to save storage
Anti-entropy protocols prevent the spread of misinformation across the network
Anti-entropy works by electing a leader to broadcast all state changes
questions.explanation: Anti-entropy: periodic full state exchange to correct gaps from missed rounds. Option 1 is about deletion. Option 2 assumes deleted data prevents spread. Option 3 requires a leader.

questions.q: In gossip-based membership protocols, what is the 'eventual membership' guarantee?
options: All nodes will eventually have consistent knowledge of which nodes are alive, though there may be temporary inconsistencies during failures or network partitions
All nodes must instantaneously agree on the current membership
Membership changes are never lost, even if they occur during a network partition
Gossip-based membership guarantees that crashed nodes are detected within one second
questions.explanation: Eventual membership: all non-faulty nodes converge on the same view after sufficient gossip rounds. Option 1 assumes perfect synchrony. Option 2 is inevitable during partitions. Option 3 assumes immediate detection.

questions.q: What is a potential weakness of gossip-based dissemination in very large networks?
options: Messages may be gossiped multiple times to the same node, creating unnecessary network traffic and amplifying overhead linearly or quadratically
Gossip protocols do not scale beyond a few hundred nodes
Gossip requires a central coordinator to manage message delivery
Gossip protocols guarantee perfect consistency across all nodes
questions.explanation: Redundancy: a node might receive the same update from multiple peers. In poorly tuned systems, this creates quadratic overhead. Option 1 is a false limit. Option 2 requires centralization. Option 3 contradicts eventual consistency.

questions.q: How can gossip protocols be optimized to reduce redundant message transmission?
options: By using techniques like bloom filters to track seen updates, digest exchanges to identify missing data before sending full payloads, and adaptive fan-out based on dissemination progress
By electing a single leader to broadcast all updates, eliminating peer-to-peer exchanges
By limiting gossip to a fixed set of 3-5 neighbors, never varying
By requiring nodes to acknowledge every received message before spreading it further
questions.explanation: Optimizations include bloom filters (compact history), digests (identify diffs), adaptive fan-out (more early, less late). Option 1 reduces gossip to broadcast. Option 2 limits flexibility. Option 3 is unnecessary.

questions.q: What happens in a gossip protocol if a network partition isolates a subset of nodes from the rest?
options: Each partition converges to its own consistent view; when the partition heals, nodes exchange updates and merge views, potentially detecting concurrent updates
All nodes immediately detect the partition and halt information dissemination
The partition is impossible in gossip protocols due to their fully distributed design
One partition automatically becomes the 'canonical' view while the other is discarded
questions.explanation: Partitioned clusters gossip internally; reunion requires merge logic (e.g., take union, use versioning). Option 1 assumes immediate detection. Option 2 is false; partitions occur in any network. Option 3 contradicts distributed decision-making.

questions.q: Why are gossip protocols valuable for managing cluster membership in eventually consistent systems?
options: Gossip-based membership is resilient to failures, requires no central authority, and allows nodes to organically learn about cluster topology without strict synchronization
Gossip protocols detect failures faster than heartbeat mechanisms
Gossip eliminates the need for any form of failure detection
Gossip-based membership guarantees that all nodes always have identical views of the cluster
questions.explanation: Gossip decentralizes membership: no single point of failure, no strict timing, adaptive to churn. Option 1 assumes faster detection. Option 2 requires detection to work. Option 3 is impossible during partitions.
